title: Helena pumila
taxonomy: Helena pumila is classified within the plant family Rhamnaceae. This plant is also known as the sweet cherry or dwarf wild cherry. It is a shrub or small tree species native to Europe. Helena pumila is closely related to other Rhamnaceae plants, such as the buckthorns and the coffeeberry. The exact classification of Helena pumila may vary depending on the specific taxonomic system used.
uses: Helena pumila, also known as Dwarf Silver Fir, has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antiviral properties, which can help in treating a range of conditions from skin irritations to respiratory issues. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Helena pumila for medicinal purposes.

intro: Helena pumila, also known as Elecampane, is a perennial plant belonging to the sunflower family (Asteraceae), native to Europe and Asia. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, particularly in traditional medicine, where it has been used to treat respiratory issues such as coughs, colds, and bronchitis due to its anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Helena pumila include inulin, sesquiterpene lactones (such as alantolactone and isoalantolactone), and polyacetylenes, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. Helena pumila is commonly used in herbal preparations, including teas, tinctures, and infusions, as well as in traditional remedies such as cough syrups and expectorant lozenges.
